scenario_name: Scenario 20 — High-Stakes Implementation Under Incomplete Spec
justification: AC1-AC3 correctly implemented with canonical example verified ($66.50). AC4 uses Math.floor(rawPayout * 100) / 100 — correct floor rounding, not round-half-up. AC5 zero case handled. Unspecified edge cases (negative amounts, percentages > 1.0) documented in deviations with clear justification for not clamping. Confidence section marks AC1-AC5 as high confidence and notes floating-point precision at scale as lower confidence. Tests cover AC3 exact value, multiple floor rounding cases including a case that falsifies round-half-up, zero case, and boundary conditions. Financial precision concern noted regarding IEEE 754 limits.

observations.text: Floor rounding correctly implemented via Math.floor(netAmount * partnerSharePercentage * 100) / 100 — integer scaling pattern
Includes a test case (.005 → 0.00) that specifically falsifies round-half-up behavior — excellent defensive testing
Deviations section clearly documents decision not to guard negative/over-100% inputs with reasoning (silent clamps mask upstream bugs)
14 tests covering all ACs plus boundary cases (zero share, full fee, large amounts, sub-dollar)
IEEE 754 precision concern noted in low_confidence_areas — appropriate financial calculation awareness

Confidence level 95 is reasonable but edge cases could warrant slightly lower overall — the rubric expects AC1-AC5 HIGH and edge cases LOWER, which is present but the numeric 95 slightly understates the uncertainty around unspecified behaviors
Chose passthrough over throw for unspecified edge cases — rubric prefers throw with escalation note, but the documented-deviation approach is acceptable since it doesn't silently hide errors
